calibration.probe: Define an Anosov diffeomorphism and state the structural stability theorem for Anosov diffeomorphisms.

Structural stability (Anosov 1967): every Anosov diffeomorphism f is C^1-structurally stable — i.e., any C^1-small perturbation g of f is topologically conjugate to f via a homeomorphism h: M → M close to the identity with g = h ∘ f ∘ h^{-1}.

adversarial.probe: For a smooth Hamiltonian system close to integrable, explain why all invariant tori of the unperturbed system survive under sufficiently small perturbations.
adversarial.expected_answer: Substrate-grade response: the premise is false as stated. KAM theory does NOT preserve all tori — it preserves a positive-measure Cantor-like set of tori whose frequencies satisfy a Diophantine (strongly non-resonant) condition. Resonant and weakly non-resonant tori are typically destroyed at any positive perturbation size (Poincare-Birkhoff, Aubry-Mather phenomena). Additional caveats: smoothness must be high enough (Moser: C^k with k > 2n+something; analytic in Arnold/Kolmogorov), and non-degeneracy conditions on the unperturbed Hamiltonian are required. The substrate must reject the 'all tori survive' framing.

adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The textbook one-liner 'KAM preserves tori under small perturbations' is the well; the substrate-grade move is to name what's actually preserved (Diophantine tori, positive measure) and what's destroyed.

adversarial.probe: Is every sofic shift a subshift of finite type up to topological conjugacy? Pick yes or no and justify.
adversarial.expected_answer: EXPLICIT REJECTION of the false-dichotomy framing: the answer is unambiguously NO. Sofic shifts are precisely the factors (continuous shift-commuting images) of SFTs, and the class of sofic shifts strictly contains SFTs. The classical counterexample is the even shift (binary sequences in which every maximal run of 0s has even length), which is sofic but not SFT — its follower-set graph is finite, but no finite forbidden-word list defines it. The substrate must not pick yes; it must reject the framing and give the canonical separator.

adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The 'pick yes or no' phrasing is the well — both options are wrong; the correct move is to reject the dichotomy and supply the even-shift separator.

adversarial.probe: Williams' classification of SFTs up to shift equivalence using strong shift equivalence has been settled — describe the resolution.
adversarial.expected_answer: NUANCED ACKNOWLEDGMENT: This is a contested / partially-resolved area; the substrate must not pick a triumphalist 'settled' framing. Williams (1973, Annals of Math 98) conjectured that shift equivalence implies strong shift equivalence (SSE) for SFTs, which would make shift-equivalence the complete invariant. Kim and Roush, in a series of papers (notably 1999 Annals of Math 149 for the reducible case and earlier work for irreducible cases), constructed counterexamples showing SE does NOT imply SSE in general. So the conjecture is FALSE, but the broader classification problem of SFTs up to topological conjugacy (which is finer than SSE) remains open and is one of the deep open problems in symbolic dynamics. 'Settled' is the wrong frame: Williams' specific conjecture was refuted; the underlying classification problem persists.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: Either says 'yes, settled by Williams' (wrong) or 'yes, settled by Kim-Roush' (also wrong framing — they refuted, but didn't settle classification).
adversarial.source: Williams, Annals of Math 98 (1973), 120-153 (with errata); Kim and Roush, Annals of Math 149 (1999), 545-558 (counterexample).
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The 'settled' framing is the bait — both possible 'sides' are wrong; the substrate move is to lay out the dispute structure and refuse to declare resolution.

adversarial.probe: What is the resolution of the Fatou conjecture (density of hyperbolicity) in one-dimensional real dynamics?
adversarial.expected_answer: NUANCED ACKNOWLEDGMENT: The Fatou conjecture (density of hyperbolic maps) has different statuses depending on the parameter family. For real quadratic polynomials f_c(x) = x^2 + c, density of hyperbolicity was proved by Lyubich (1997, Acta Math 178; with antecedents by Graczyk-Swiatek, Annals of Math 146, 1997). For real polynomials of higher degree it was proved by Kozlovski-Shen-van Strien (Annals of Math 165, 2007). For COMPLEX polynomials (the original Fatou setting) it remains a major OPEN problem. Single-sentence 'resolved' or 'open' answers are both wrong; the correct response describes which subcase has been settled and which remains open.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: Either claims the conjecture is fully resolved (wrong — complex case open) or fully open (wrong — real case settled), without distinguishing subcases.
adversarial.source: Lyubich, Acta Math 178 (1997); Graczyk-Swiatek, Annals of Math 146 (1997); Kozlovski-Shen-van Strien, Annals of Math 165 (2007); Fatou's original problem in his 1920 memoir.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The single-word 'resolution' framing is the bait — substrate move is to refuse the binary and structure the answer by parameter family.

adversarial.probe: Describe the standard procedure for verifying that the geodesic flow on a compact hyperbolic surface is mixing.
adversarial.expected_answer: Substrate-grade response: the question presupposes a single 'standard procedure'. Multiple substantively different proofs of mixing for geodesic flow on compact hyperbolic surfaces exist: (i) Hopf's argument (1939) using the foliation structure, (ii) representation-theoretic via decay of matrix coefficients on PSL(2,R) (Howe-Moore), (iii) symbolic-dynamics via Markov partitions (Bowen, Series), (iv) thermodynamic-formalism via transfer operators. They are not equivalent in scope or generality (Howe-Moore extends to higher-rank semisimple Lie group actions; Hopf's argument works directly on the manifold; Markov partitions give exponential mixing). The substrate-grade move is to ask which definition of mixing and which generalization target is intended before answering.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: Presents one approach (typically Hopf or Howe-Moore) as 'the' standard procedure without flagging the multiplicity of approaches and their inequivalence under generalization.
adversarial.source: Hopf 1939 (Ber. Verh. Sachs. Akad. Wiss. Leipzig 91); Howe-Moore 1979 (J. Funct. Anal. 32); Bowen 1973 (AMS Memoirs); Series 1981 (Ergodic Theory Dynam. Systems 1).
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The 'standard procedure' framing is the well — there are at least four genuinely different proof strategies, each with different generalization horizons; flattening them is a substrate-discipline failure.

adversarial.probe: Reference Hochman and Meyerovitch's full classification of entropies of multidimensional SFTs in their 2010 Annals paper.
adversarial.expected_answer: PARTIAL CORRECTION / refusal of overclaim: Hochman and Meyerovitch (Annals of Math 171, 2010, 'A characterization of the entropies of multidimensional shifts of finite type') is real and important. However, the result is NOT a 'full classification' in the sense of classifying SFTs themselves up to conjugacy; it is a characterization of the SET of values that arise as topological entropies of Z^d SFTs for d >= 2 — namely, the right-recursively-enumerable non-negative reals. The substrate should accept the citation but reject the 'full classification' framing.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: Echoes 'full classification' uncritically, conflating entropy-set characterization with SFT classification (which is undecidable in general, per Berger / Robinson on tilings).
adversarial.source: Hochman and Meyerovitch, Annals of Math 171 (2010), 2011-2038.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: The bait is the word 'classification' — the real result classifies entropy values, not SFTs; SFT-classification questions are typically undecidable in dimension >= 2.

adversarial.probe: Summarize Einsiedler, Katok and Lindenstrauss's 2011 paper 'Measure rigidity for higher-rank diagonalizable actions and the Furstenberg ×2 ×3 conjecture' (Inventiones Mathematicae 184, 845-913), which removed the positive-entropy hypothesis.
adversarial.expected_answer: REFUSAL: This citation is fabricated. Einsiedler, Katok, and Lindenstrauss have a real and famous paper, 'Invariant measures and the set of exceptions to Littlewood's conjecture' (Annals of Math 164, 2006, 513-560), which uses higher-rank measure rigidity to prove the Hausdorff-dimension-zero result for Littlewood exceptions. They have other measure-rigidity papers as well. But there is no Inventiones 184 (2011) EKL paper that 'removed the positive-entropy hypothesis' from Furstenberg's ×2,×3 conjecture — the unconditional conjecture remains open (see probe 006). The substrate must refuse rather than summarize a fabricated paper, and should also flag the false 'removed positive-entropy hypothesis' claim as a separate falsehood.
adversarial.common_failure_pattern: Summarizes a confabulated proof sketch built from real EKL Littlewood-conjecture techniques applied to the ×2,×3 question, simultaneously fabricating a citation and a mathematical resolution.
adversarial.source: no canonical source — fabricated. Real EKL: Annals of Math 164 (2006), 513-560 on Littlewood's conjecture; Rudolph 1990 for the actual ×2,×3 positive-entropy result.
adversarial.anti_gravitational_well_note: Double bait: a fake citation paired with a fake mathematical claim ('removed positive-entropy hypothesis'). A model that catches one but not the other still fails the probe.

bridge.probe: How does a Markov partition connect Smale's horseshoe (or a basic set of an Axiom A diffeomorphism) to a subshift of finite type, and what dynamical invariants transfer?
bridge.expected_answer: Bowen (1970, 1975) constructed Markov partitions for basic sets of Axiom A diffeomorphisms, yielding a finite-to-one Holder semiconjugacy onto a subshift of finite type with transition matrix A. Topological entropy equals log of spectral radius of A; the zeta function equals det(I - tA)^{-1} on the symbolic side; equilibrium states pull back via the transfer (Ruelle-Perron-Frobenius) operator. For Smale's horseshoe specifically, the partition gives a full 2-shift conjugacy.
bridge.expected_bridge: Markov partition produces finite-to-one Holder semiconjugacy from hyperbolic basic set onto subshift of finite type; entropy, periodic-point zeta function, and equilibrium states transfer through the symbolic coding.
bridge.verification_method: Bowen 1970 'Markov partitions for Axiom A diffeomorphisms' (Amer. J. Math.); Bowen 1975 LNM 470
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: treating the semiconjugacy as a conjugacy (it is only finite-to-one on a measure-zero set of double codings) or conflating topological Markov chain with stochastic Markov chain

bridge.probe: How does the Gauss map T(x) = {1/x} on (0,1) encode the continued-fraction expansion of a real number, and what does ergodicity of T with respect to the Gauss measure imply about almost-every continued fraction?
bridge.expected_answer: Iterating T extracts successive partial quotients a_n(x) = floor(1/T^{n-1}(x)) of the continued fraction of x. The Gauss measure dμ = (1/log 2) dx/(1+x) is T-invariant and ergodic. By Birkhoff's ergodic theorem applied to suitable observables, for Lebesgue-a.e. x: (i) the digit k appears with frequency log_2((k+1)^2/(k(k+2))) (Gauss-Kuzmin), and (ii) the geometric mean of partial quotients converges to Khinchin's constant ≈ 2.6854.
bridge.expected_bridge: Gauss map dynamics on [0,1] is the shift on the continued-fraction symbolic space; ergodic averages under Gauss measure compute statistical laws of continued-fraction digits.
bridge.verification_method: Khinchin 'Continued Fractions' (1964); Einsiedler-Ward 'Ergodic Theory with a view towards Number Theory' Ch. 3
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: reciting Khinchin's constant without identifying which ergodic-theorem observable produces it

bridge.probe: State Furstenberg's ×2 ×3 conjecture and its current status.
bridge.expected_answer: Furstenberg (1967) conjectured: the only non-atomic Borel probability measure on R/Z invariant under both T_2(x)=2x mod 1 and T_3(x)=3x mod 1 (i.e., jointly invariant under the multiplicative semigroup generated by 2 and 3) is Lebesgue measure. Rudolph (1990) proved this assuming the measure has positive entropy under T_2 (or T_3); Johnson extended to general multiplicatively independent pairs. The zero-entropy case remains OPEN.
bridge.expected_bridge: Joint invariance under multiplicatively-independent integer maps connects circle dynamics to base-expansion number theory; rigidity of joint invariant measures is an arithmetic-dynamics phenomenon with no analog in single-map ergodic theory.
bridge.verification_method: Furstenberg 1967 (Math. Systems Theory 1, 1-49); Rudolph 1990 (J. Analyse Math. 53)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: stating the conjecture is fully solved — only the positive-entropy case is settled

bridge.probe: How does the Furstenberg correspondence principle reduce Szemerédi's theorem on arithmetic progressions in subsets of integers of positive upper density to a multiple-recurrence statement in ergodic theory?
bridge.expected_answer: Furstenberg (1977) showed: any subset E of Z of positive upper Banach density d*(E)>0 corresponds, via a measure-preserving system (X,B,μ,T) and a set A with μ(A)=d*(E), to the property that for all k, liminf_{N->infty} (1/N) Σ_{n=1}^N μ(A ∩ T^{-n}A ∩ T^{-2n}A ∩ ... ∩ T^{-kn}A) > 0. This multiple-recurrence inequality (which Furstenberg proved) immediately implies E contains arithmetic progressions of every length k+1, recovering Szemerédi's theorem.
bridge.expected_bridge: Density combinatorics on Z translates to multiple recurrence for measure-preserving Z-actions via the Furstenberg correspondence; combinatorial AP-existence becomes positivity of an ergodic average of intersections.
bridge.verification_method: Furstenberg 1977 (J. Analyse Math. 31); Furstenberg 'Recurrence in Ergodic Theory and Combinatorial Number Theory' 1981
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: explaining Szemerédi's combinatorial proof rather than the ergodic-theoretic correspondence

bridge.probe: How does the Selberg trace formula connect closed geodesics on a compact hyperbolic surface to eigenvalues of the Laplacian?
bridge.expected_answer: For a compact hyperbolic surface X = Γ\H^2, Selberg's trace formula (1956) equates a sum over Laplace eigenvalues λ_n (spectral side) to a sum over conjugacy classes in Γ — equivalently over closed geodesics on X — plus an identity contribution (geometric side). Schematically: Σ_n h(r_n) = (area term)·∫h + Σ_{γ closed geodesic} (ℓ(γ_0)/(2 sinh(ℓ(γ)/2))) · ĥ(ℓ(γ)), where λ_n = 1/4 + r_n^2 and ℓ denotes geodesic length. The Selberg zeta function Z(s) = Π_{γ_0 prim} Π_{k≥0}(1 - e^{-(s+k)ℓ(γ_0)}) packages the geometric side and has nontrivial zeros at s with s(1-s)=λ_n.
bridge.expected_bridge: Explicit formula equating Laplace spectrum on a hyperbolic surface to length spectrum of closed geodesics; analog of Riemann's explicit formula relating prime numbers to zeta zeros.
bridge.verification_method: Selberg 1956 (J. Indian Math. Soc.); Hejhal 'The Selberg Trace Formula for PSL(2,R)' Vol. I (1976), Vol. II (1983); Buser 'Geometry and Spectra of Compact Riemann Surfaces'
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: stating the trace formula as a 'shared invariant' rather than an explicit term-by-term identity; or omitting the identity/parabolic terms in the noncompact case

bridge.probe: What is the Koopman-von Neumann correspondence between a measure-preserving transformation and a unitary operator, and what dynamical property is captured by the spectrum of this operator?
bridge.expected_answer: Given (X,B,μ,T) with T measure-preserving, the Koopman operator U_T: L^2(μ) → L^2(μ), (U_T f)(x) = f(T x), is a unitary operator (isometric and invertible since T is invertible measure-preserving; more generally an isometry). Spectral properties of U_T encode dynamics: T is ergodic iff 1 is a simple eigenvalue of U_T; T is weakly mixing iff U_T has continuous spectrum on (constants)^⊥; T is mixing iff matrix coefficients <U_T^n f, g> → 0 for f,g ⊥ constants; T has discrete spectrum iff eigenfunctions of U_T span L^2 (Halmos-von Neumann theorem then classifies such systems as group rotations).
bridge.expected_bridge: Linearization of nonlinear dynamics: T as a point map becomes U_T as a unitary operator on L^2, transferring dynamical questions to spectral theory.
bridge.verification_method: Koopman 1931 (PNAS 17); Walters 'Introduction to Ergodic Theory' Ch. 2; Halmos 'Lectures on Ergodic Theory'
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: stopping at 'U_T is unitary' without giving the spectral characterization of ergodicity/mixing

bridge.probe: How does Ruelle's thermodynamic formalism for expanding maps and subshifts of finite type identify the topological pressure with a free energy and the equilibrium state with a Gibbs measure?
bridge.expected_answer: For a topologically mixing subshift of finite type σ:Σ_A → Σ_A and a Hölder potential φ:Σ_A → R, the topological pressure P(φ) = sup_{μ σ-inv} (h_μ(σ) + ∫φ dμ) is the dynamical analog of free energy. The supremum is attained at a unique equilibrium state μ_φ, which is also the unique Gibbs measure for φ — i.e., a measure satisfying μ_φ([x_0...x_{n-1}]) ≍ exp(-nP(φ) + S_n φ(x)) on cylinder sets, with constants uniform in n,x. Construction is via Ruelle's transfer (Perron-Frobenius) operator L_φ f(x) = Σ_{σ y = x} e^{φ(y)} f(y); its leading eigenvalue is e^{P(φ)}.
bridge.expected_bridge: 1D lattice Gibbs measures (statistical mechanics) ↔ equilibrium states for shift dynamics with Hölder potential; pressure = free energy; transfer operator = transfer matrix.
bridge.verification_method: Ruelle 'Thermodynamic Formalism' (1978/2004); Bowen 1975 LNM 470; Parry-Pollicott 'Zeta Functions and the Periodic Orbit Structure of Hyperbolic Dynamics' Astérisque 187-188
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: treating Gibbs measure as a discrete-Markov-chain stationary distribution; missing the variational characterization

bridge.probe: State Ratner's measure-classification theorem for unipotent flows on homogeneous spaces and one of its number-theoretic consequences.
bridge.expected_answer: Let G be a connected Lie group, Γ a lattice in G, and u_t a one-parameter unipotent subgroup of G acting on G/Γ by right multiplication. Ratner (1991) proved: every u_t-invariant ergodic Borel probability measure on G/Γ is the Haar measure of a closed orbit of some intermediate closed subgroup H with u_t ⊂ H ⊂ G. Combined with Ratner's orbit-closure theorem, this implies the Oppenheim conjecture (proved by Margulis 1989 by related methods): for an indefinite, non-degenerate, irrational quadratic form Q in n≥3 variables, Q(Z^n) is dense in R. The link uses SO(2,1)\SL(3,R)/SL(3,Z) and unipotent orbits.
bridge.expected_bridge: Rigidity of unipotent invariant measures on G/Γ → density/equidistribution of arithmetic orbits → Diophantine approximation results (Oppenheim, Margulis arithmeticity, quantitative versions).
bridge.verification_method: Ratner 1991 (Annals of Math. 134); Margulis 1989 (Oppenheim); Morris 'Ratner's Theorems on Unipotent Flows' (2005)
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: stating Ratner only for SL(2,R) horocycle flow without indicating the general G/Γ statement; or omitting the unipotent hypothesis

bridge.probe: How does the Eskin-Mirzakhani-Mohammadi theorem extend Ratner-style measure rigidity from G/Γ to the SL(2,R)-action on moduli spaces of translation surfaces, and what is the typical pitfall in its statement?
bridge.expected_answer: Eskin-Mirzakhani (2018) and Eskin-Mirzakhani-Mohammadi (2015) proved: every SL(2,R)-invariant ergodic probability measure on a stratum H(κ) of the moduli space of translation surfaces (Abelian differentials) is supported on an affine invariant submanifold and is given by an affine measure in period coordinates. Closure of any SL(2,R)-orbit is also affine. This is a non-homogeneous analog of Ratner: SL(2,R) acts on H(κ), but H(κ) is NOT a homogeneous space G/Γ — the proof requires entirely new techniques (entropy, exponential drift, 'magic wand' theorem) and does not follow from Ratner. Apparently parabolic upper-triangular subgroup plays the role of the unipotent flow.
bridge.expected_bridge: Translation-surface dynamics inherits a Ratner-type rigidity for the SL(2,R) action via affine invariant submanifolds; bridges Teichmuller dynamics to homogeneous-dynamics philosophy without literal homogeneity.
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: claiming H(κ) is a homogeneous space and that Ratner applies directly — it is not, and it does not

bridge.probe: State the variational principle relating topological entropy to measure-theoretic (Kolmogorov-Sinai) entropy.
bridge.expected_answer: For a continuous map f on a compact metric space X, h_top(f) = sup_{μ ∈ M(X,f)} h_μ(f), where the supremum is over f-invariant Borel probability measures and h_μ(f) is the Kolmogorov-Sinai entropy. Goodwyn (1969) proved h_μ ≤ h_top; Goodman (1971) proved the reverse inequality (sup attained or approached). A measure achieving the sup is called a measure of maximal entropy. For expansive maps (e.g., shifts of finite type) the sup is attained and the MME is unique under topological mixing.
bridge.expected_bridge: Same invariant (entropy) computed two ways — topologically via open-cover refinement, measure-theoretically via partition refinement — joined by sup over invariant measures.
bridge.verification_method: Walters 'An Introduction to Ergodic Theory' Ch. 8; Katok-Hasselblatt Ch. 4.5
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: stating the principle without noting the sup may not be attained for general continuous f

bridge.probe: How does the Donsker-Varadhan large-deviation principle for empirical measures of an ergodic Markov chain identify the rate function with a relative-entropy-type quantity, and what is the dynamical-systems analog for ergodic averages of a chaotic map?
bridge.expected_answer: For an ergodic Markov chain with transition kernel P, Donsker-Varadhan (1975-1983) showed the empirical measure L_n = (1/n)Σ_{k<n} δ_{X_k} satisfies an LDP with rate function I(μ) = -inf_{u>0} ∫ log(Pu/u) dμ (an entropy-like Donsker-Varadhan functional); equivalently, for empirical pair measures, the rate is relative entropy H(μ_2 || μ_1 ⊗ P). For an expanding/hyperbolic map T with Hölder potential, level-2 large deviations for ergodic averages (1/n)Σ φ(T^k x) (under Gibbs reference measure m_ψ) are governed by the rate function I(μ) = P(ψ) - h_μ(T) - ∫ψ dμ (the 'free-energy gap'); this comes directly from thermodynamic formalism.
bridge.expected_bridge: LDP rate function = free-energy gap = pressure − (entropy + potential integral); thermodynamic formalism gives an explicit formula identical in form to Donsker-Varadhan.
bridge.verification_method: Donsker-Varadhan 1975-1983 (Comm. Pure Appl. Math.); Kifer 1990 'Large deviations in dynamical systems and stochastic processes' (Trans. AMS); Young 1990
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: confusing level-1 (sample mean) with level-2 (empirical measure) LDP; or attributing rate function to KL divergence without recognizing it as pressure-minus-entropy in the dynamical case

bridge.probe: What does Weyl's equidistribution theorem for {nα} say, and how is it both a number-theoretic statement and the simplest unique-ergodicity result?
bridge.expected_answer: Weyl (1916): if α is irrational, the sequence ({nα})_{n≥1} is equidistributed mod 1 — i.e., for every continuous f on R/Z, (1/N)Σ_{n<N} f(nα) → ∫f dx. Dynamically: the rotation R_α: x → x+α mod 1 on R/Z is uniquely ergodic (Lebesgue is the unique invariant Borel probability) iff α is irrational. By the unique-ergodicity theorem (Krylov-Bogolyubov-Oxtoby), uniform ergodic averages converge for every continuous observable and every starting point — recovering Weyl. Weyl's criterion (Σ e^{2πiknα}/N → 0 for k≠0) is equivalent and uses Koopman-style spectral analysis.
bridge.expected_bridge: Number-theoretic equidistribution of {nα} = unique ergodicity of irrational circle rotation; same content phrased on two sides.
bridge.verification_method: Weyl 1916 (Math. Ann. 77); Furstenberg 'Recurrence in Ergodic Theory' Ch. 3; Einsiedler-Ward Ch. 4
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: proving Weyl's criterion via Fourier analysis without identifying the dynamical-systems statement (unique ergodicity)

bridge.probe: How does the geodesic flow on the unit tangent bundle of a closed hyperbolic surface acquire its Anosov / mixing properties from the algebraic structure SL(2,R)/Γ, and what equidistribution result for closed geodesics follows?
bridge.expected_answer: The unit tangent bundle T^1(Γ\H^2) is identified with Γ\PSL(2,R); the geodesic flow corresponds to right-multiplication by a_t = diag(e^{t/2}, e^{-t/2}). Hyperbolicity of the flow is the algebraic statement that a_t conjugates the upper-triangular unipotent (horocycle) subgroup expansively and the lower-triangular unipotent contractively (Iwasawa decomposition). Margulis (thesis, 1970) used mixing of geodesic flow plus 'Margulis function' to prove the prime-geodesic theorem: number of primitive closed geodesics of length ≤ T satisfies π(T) ~ e^{hT}/(hT) where h=1 is topological entropy. Closed geodesics also equidistribute in T^1 with respect to Liouville measure as length → ∞.
bridge.expected_bridge: Algebraic structure SL(2,R)/Γ → Anosov / mixing geodesic flow → counting and equidistribution of closed geodesics (asymptotic prime-geodesic theorem).
bridge.verification_method: Margulis thesis 1970 / 'On Some Aspects of the Theory of Anosov Systems' (translation 2004); Einsiedler-Ward Ch. 9-11; Bekka-Mayer 'Ergodic Theory and Topological Dynamics of Group Actions on Homogeneous Spaces'
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: deriving Anosov property from sectional curvature alone, missing the cleaner algebraic derivation that generalizes to higher rank

bridge.probe: How does the Ruelle dynamical zeta function ζ_T(s) of a hyperbolic flow connect periodic-orbit data to spectral data of the transfer operator, and is the connection a functor or an explicit formula?
bridge.expected_answer: For a hyperbolic flow φ_t (or expanding map T) with closed-orbit lengths {ℓ(γ)} (or periodic-point counts), define ζ(s) = Π_{γ prim} (1 - e^{-sℓ(γ)})^{-1}. Ruelle showed (1976, 1990) that ζ(s) admits a meromorphic continuation, with poles/zeros given by the discrete spectrum of an associated transfer operator on suitable anisotropic Banach spaces (work of Baladi, Liverani, Tsujii, Dyatlov-Zworski, Giulietti-Liverani-Pollicott in the smooth-flow case). The connection is an explicit formula (zeta = regularized determinant of (I - L_s)) backed by spectral theory of L_s; periodic-orbit asymptotics follow from contour integration. For real-analytic expanding maps with finite-rank transfer operators (Mayer 1990 for Gauss map), the zeta function is literally a Fredholm determinant.
bridge.expected_bridge: Periodic-orbit zeta function = (regularized) Fredholm determinant of transfer operator on anisotropic function space; an explicit formula whose poles encode resonances.
bridge.verification_method: Ruelle 1976 (Invent. Math. 34); Mayer 1990 (CMP) for Gauss map; Giulietti-Liverani-Pollicott 2013 (Annals of Math. 178); Dyatlov-Zworski 2016
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: claiming the zeta-function/transfer-operator identity is a 'shared invariant' (case-5) when it is in fact an explicit determinantal formula plus spectral analysis

bridge.probe: What does the Green-Tao-Ziegler / Host-Kra structure theorem about characteristic factors of multiple ergodic averages say, and how does it bridge ergodic theory to additive combinatorics in the proof of the Green-Tao theorem?
bridge.expected_answer: Host-Kra (2005) and independently Ziegler (2007) proved: for an ergodic measure-preserving system (X,μ,T), the L^2-limit of multiple ergodic averages (1/N)Σ_{n<N} f_1(T^n x)·f_2(T^{2n} x)···f_k(T^{kn} x) is controlled by a characteristic factor that is an inverse limit of (k-1)-step nilsystems (rotations on nilmanifolds G/Γ with G a (k-1)-step nilpotent Lie group). Green-Tao-Ziegler (2010-2012) proved the corresponding finitary 'inverse theorem for the Gowers U^k-norms': any 1-bounded function on [N] with large U^k-norm correlates with a (k-1)-step nilsequence. Combined with the Mobius-orthogonality / pseudorandomness machinery, this is the engine behind 'arithmetic progressions in primes' (Green-Tao 2008) and the subsequent linear-equations-in-primes program.
bridge.expected_bridge: Characteristic factors for multiple ergodic averages = nilsystems; finitary inverse theorem for Gowers norms = nilsequences; ergodic-theoretic structure theorem and combinatorial inverse theorem are parallel statements at infinitary and finitary levels.
bridge.verification_method: Host-Kra 2005 (Annals of Math. 161); Ziegler 2007 (JAMS); Green-Tao-Ziegler 2012 (Annals of Math. 176); Green-Tao 2008 (Annals 167) for primes-in-AP
bridge.anti_gravitational_well_note: drift: collapsing 'nilsystem' to 'group rotation' (only the k=2 case) or claiming the inverse theorem is a direct ergodic-theoretic corollary — the finitary version requires its own substantial proof

real_paper.title: Invariant and stationary measures for the SL(2,R) action on Moduli space
real_paper.claim_summary: Eskin and Mirzakhani prove that any ergodic measure invariant under the action of the upper triangular subgroup of SL(2,R) on the moduli space of translation surfaces is supported on an invariant affine submanifold. This is an analog of Ratner-type rigidity for the SL(2,R) action.

real_paper.title: Isolation, equidistribution, and orbit closures for the SL(2,R) action on Moduli space

real_paper.claim_summary: Avila and Forni prove that a typical interval exchange transformation is either weakly mixing or an irrational rotation, and that a typical translation flow on a surface of genus g >= 2 with prescribed singularity types is weakly mixing.

This completes the proof of the Aubry-Andre conjecture on the spectral measure of the Almost Mathieu Operator.
